---
title: "Appendix: The Bias‚ÄìVariance Decomposition"
subtitle: "INFO 521 ¬∑ Module 2 ¬∑ Lecture B ‚Äî supplement"
author: "Greg Chism"
institute: "School of Information ¬∑ University of Arizona"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---

## Where we are {.smaller}

Lecture B stated that, at a fixed input $\mathbf{x}$, expected test error splits into
three pieces:

$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2\bigr]
\;=\; \text{bias}^2 \;+\; \text{variance} \;+\; \sigma^2 .
$$

This appendix derives that in full. Bookkeeping first ‚Äî at the fixed $\mathbf{x}$:

- **Truth**: $y = f(\mathbf{x}) + \varepsilon$, with
  $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$, $\ \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] = \sigma^2$.
- **Estimate**: $\hat{f} = \hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}}(\mathbf{x})$ ‚Äî random *through the
  training set* $\mathcal{D}$ (m3b's repeated-datasets picture, in prediction space).
- **Average fit**: $\bar{f} = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}]$ ‚Äî the orange curve from
  Lecture B's repeated-fits figure.
- $\varepsilon$ is a **new** test-time noise draw, independent of $\mathcal{D}$.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: optional depth for students who want the algebra. Everything hangs on one
trick ‚Äî add and subtract $\bar{f}$ ‚Äî and two cross terms dying.
:::

## The one trick: add and subtract $\bar{f}$ {.smaller}

Write the miss as three centered pieces:

$$
y - \hat{f}
= \underbrace{\bigl(f - \bar{f}\bigr)}_{\text{systematic miss}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\bigl(\bar{f} - \hat{f}\bigr)}_{\text{dataset luck}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\varepsilon}_{\text{fresh noise}} .
$$

::: {.fragment}
Square and take the expectation (over $\mathcal{D}$ *and* $\varepsilon$):

$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(y - \hat{f})^2\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}\bigl[(f - \bar{f})^2\bigr]
+ \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})^2\bigr]
+ \mathbb{E}\bigl[\varepsilon^2\bigr]
+ \text{(three cross terms)} .
$$
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: $(a+b+c)^2 = a^2+b^2+c^2+2ab+2ac+2bc$ ‚Äî nothing deeper. The work is showing
the cross terms vanish; that's the next slide.
:::

## The cross terms die {.smaller}

Take each in turn:

::: {.fragment}
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(f - \bar{f})\,(\bar{f} - \hat{f})\bigr]
= (f - \bar{f})\;\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\bigl[\bar{f} - \hat{f}\bigr]
= (f - \bar{f}) \cdot 0 = 0
$$

‚Äî $f - \bar{f}$ is a **constant** (no randomness left), and $\hat{f}$ is centered on
$\bar{f}$ *by definition*.
:::

::: {.fragment}
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(f - \bar{f})\,\varepsilon\bigr] = (f - \bar{f})\,\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0,
\qquad
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})\,\varepsilon\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\bigl[\bar{f} - \hat{f}\bigr]\,\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0
$$

‚Äî the test-time noise is mean-zero and **independent of the training set**, so both
$\varepsilon$ cross terms factor and vanish.
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: the independence assumption earns its keep exactly here ‚Äî if training data
leaked into the test draw, the last factorization would fail. Leakage (m2a) breaks the
theorem, not just the estimate.
:::

## The decomposition, earned {.smaller}

What survives:

$$
\boxed{\;
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2\bigr]
= \underbrace{\bigl(f(\mathbf{x}) - \bar{f}(\mathbf{x})\bigr)^2}_{\text{bias}^2}
+ \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\bigl[(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) - \bar{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2\bigr]}_{\text{variance}}
+ \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible}}
\;}
$$

- **bias¬≤** ‚Äî how far the *average* fit sits from the truth: rigidity's price.
- **variance** ‚Äî how much a single fit swings around that average: flexibility's price.
- **$\sigma^2$** ‚Äî the noise floor; no model, however tuned, goes below it.

[Complexity moves the first two in opposite directions; their sum is the U-curve of
Lectures A/B. Ridge's $\lambda$ walks you along it.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: point back to m2b's repeated-fits figure ‚Äî degree 1 was the bias¬≤ term made
visible; degree 9 was the variance term. The figure and this box are the same statement.
:::

## Fine print {.smaller}

Three things the derivation quietly fixed ‚Äî say them out loud once:

- **One $\mathbf{x}$ at a time.** The decomposition holds pointwise; "test error" in
  practice averages it over the input distribution.
- **Squared loss only.** The clean three-way split is a property of the square; other
  losses decompose differently (or not at all).
- **Expectations, not your dataset.** Bias and variance describe the *procedure* across
  repeated datasets ‚Äî on your one sample you see a single draw, which is why CV (m2a)
  estimates rather than computes them.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: the third bullet closes the loop to m2a ‚Äî CV is how the ideal quantities in
this appendix get estimated from one real dataset.
:::
